title: Handling Medical & Legal Emergencies
learningGoal: Learn critical life-saving language to handle hospital visits, describe medical symptoms, and make police reports.
instructions: Study the essential vocabulary and sentence structures needed for emergencies. Pay special attention to how symptoms are described using 'mujhe' (to me) and how incidents are reported using passive verbs.

lessonContent.bodyText: When you face a medical emergency, the most important thing is to get to a doctor or hospital quickly. The word for hospital is 'aspataal' and doctor is usually just 'doctor'. You can ask for help immediately using imperatives.
In Hindi, symptoms are often described as something happening 'to' you. You use 'Mujhe' (To me) + Symptom + 'hai' (is). Pain is 'dard', fever is 'bukhar', and cold is 'zukham'.

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I have a very bad headache.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: मुझे कल रात से बुखार है।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Mujhe kal raat se bukhar hai.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I have a fever since last night.
There is pain in my stomach. (My stomach hurts.)

lessonContent.bodyText: If you are a victim of a crime such as theft, you must report it to the police. Theft is 'chori'. Notice the use of the passive voice: 'chori ho gayi' (theft happened / it got stolen).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: मेरा सामान चोरी हो गया है।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Mera saaman chori ho gaya hai.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: मुझे पुलिस में शिकायत दर्ज करनी है।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Mujhe police mein shikayat darj karni hai.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I want to file a police complaint.

lessonContent.bodyText: To describe an accident, the word 'durghatna' or the English word 'accident' is used. The verb 'ho jana' (to happen/occur) is common here.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: वहाँ एक एक्सीडेंट हो गया है।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Wahan ek accident ho gaya hai.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: कृपया मेरी मदद कीजिए, मैं खो गया हूँ।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Kripya meri madad kijiye, main kho gaya hoon.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Please help me, I am lost.

lessonContent.bodyText: In India, knowing the emergency numbers is crucial. 112 is the National Emergency Number, 100 is for Police, and 108 is for Ambulance.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: पुलिस को बुलाने के लिए सौ (१००) डायल करें।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Police ko bulane ke liye sau (100) dial karein.

learningObjective: Learners will confidently articulate medical symptoms, request urgent assistance, and report thefts or accidents in Hindi.

examples.explanation: Uses 'mujhe' (to me) and the continuous verb 'aa rahe hain' (are coming) for dizziness.

examples.targetText: मेरा पासपोर्ट चोरी हो गया है।
examples.transliteration: Mera passport chori ho gaya hai.
examples.explanation: Uses 'chori ho gaya' (got stolen) to indicate a passive incident.

importantRules.ruleText: Use 'मुझे' (Mujhe - To me) when talking about internal feelings like pain, fever, or dizziness (e.g., मुझे दर्द है).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: For body parts, use 'मेरे [Body Part] में दर्द है' (In my [Body Part] there is pain).

importantRules.ruleText: For incidents out of your control like theft or accidents, use the compound verb 'हो जाना' (ho jana - to happen).

cultureContext.contextText: When reporting a crime in India, you file an FIR (First Information Report) at the local police station (Thana). For medical needs, both government and private hospitals exist; private hospitals usually offer faster service but require payment upfront.
dosList: Keep digital copies of your passport and visa.
Ask locals for the nearest 'Aspataal' (Hospital) or 'Dawakhana' (Pharmacy) in an emergency.
dontsList: Don't panic; English is widely understood in Indian hospitals and police stations in cities.
Don't leave the police station without a copy of your FIR if something is stolen.

summary: This lesson covers the critical language needed for emergencies. You learned how to describe illnesses using 'mujhe', request an ambulance or doctor, and report thefts or accidents to the police using passive and compound verbs.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you say 'I have a fever' in Hindi?
What verb form is used for 'got stolen' in Hindi?

keyTakeaways: Use 'Mere [body part] mein dard hai' for localized pain.
Theft is 'chori', and 'chori ho gaya' means it was stolen.
112 is the primary emergency number in India.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastering the vocabulary and grammar for medical and legal emergencies, focusing on symptoms and incident reporting.
importantFacts: Bukhar means fever, dard means pain.
FIR (First Information Report) is the standard police complaint.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: The learner should be able to describe their symptoms to a doctor and explain an emergency or theft to authorities.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You now know the crucial phrases to handle an emergency in India.
feedbackRules.onFail: Please review the lesson again. These phrases are very important for your safety.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Make sure you memorize the words for pain and theft.

commonMistakes: Saying 'Main bukhar hoon' (I am a fever) instead of 'Mujhe bukhar hai' (To me there is a fever).
Not using 'mein' when talking about pain in a body part (e.g., saying 'Mera pet dard hai' instead of 'Mere pet mein dard hai').
